_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Robertson County, TN. Formed 1796. No major courthouse fire. Records survive from formation. Dark-fire tobacco economy generated distinctive agricultural and commercial records.

previous_courthouses.fate: Brick courthouse. Survived the Civil War and served for a century.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or destruction
record_losses.description: Robertson County's courthouse has not suffered a major fire, and records survive continuously from the 1796 formation. The county's dark-fire tobacco economy generated particularly detailed commercial records — warehouse receipts, crop lien filings, and tobacco auction records that supplement standard county records.
record_losses.narrative_impact: {ancestor_name}'s records in Robertson County survive from 1796 forward. The county's role as the center of the Black Patch tobacco economy means that agricultural and commercial records are unusually detailed — tobacco warehouse receipts and crop lien filings document the economic life of farmers between census years.
record_losses.source: Tennessee State Library and Archives. Guide to County Records.

deeds.repository: Robertson County Register of Deeds; Tennessee State Library and Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com Tennessee land records

wills_estates.completeness: Good — estate records from the early period
wills_estates.gaps: Minor gaps in earliest loose papers.
wills_estates.repository: Robertson County Probate Court; Tennessee State Library and Archives

marriages.completeness: Good — bonds from the early period; licenses from 1838
marriages.gaps: Minor gaps in earliest bond records.
marriages.repository: Robertson County Clerk; Tennessee State Library and Archives

court_minutes.completeness: Good — continuous series from formation with minor wartime disruption
court_minutes.gaps: Some disruption during Civil War. Black Patch War era (1905-1909) court records include Night Rider prosecutions.

substitute_sources.description: Dark-fire tobacco warehouses in Springfield and the surrounding towns maintained sales records, grading records, and buyer accounts. Where these survive, they document farmers' names, quantities, and prices — an annual economic snapshot.
substitute_sources.repository: Robertson County Historical Society; Tennessee State Library and Archives; some in private hands
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Tobacco warehouse receipts can document a farmer's presence in the county and economic status between census years. The quantity and grade of tobacco sold indicates relative prosperity.
substitute_sources.source: Waldrep, Night Riders. Duke University Press, 1993.

substitute_sources.description: Night Rider prosecutions and civil suits generated detailed court records that document farmers' names, relationships, economic positions, and community alliances during the Black Patch conflict.
substitute_sources.repository: Robertson County Clerk of Court; Tennessee State Library and Archives; published in Waldrep's Night Riders
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The Night Rider trials document community networks in extraordinary detail — witnesses, defendants, and victims are identified by name, occupation, farm location, and often family relationship.
substitute_sources.source: Waldrep, Christopher. Night Riders. Duke University Press, 1993.

substitute_sources.source_type: Church records — Baptist, Methodist, Churches of Christ
substitute_sources.description: Robertson County's churches maintained records. Churches of Christ (Stone-Campbell movement) were particularly strong in the Black Patch region.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual congregations; Tennessee State Library and Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Supplement civil records for vital events before 1908.
substitute_sources.source: Tennessee State Library and Archives. Guide to Church Records.

narrative_hooks: Robertson County's records survive from 1796 — the tobacco warehouse receipts and crop lien filings document {ancestor_name}'s economic life in a level of detail that standard county records rarely match.

If {ancestor_name} was caught up in the Black Patch War, the Night Rider trial records at the courthouse may document {pronoun_possessive} alliances, grievances, and neighbors in more detail than any other source.
The Dark Patch tobacco warehouses kept their own books — the receipt for {ancestor_name}'s tobacco crop tells a story of grade, weight, and price that the census never captured.
Robertson County records are intact from formation — when they go silent about {ancestor_name}, the silence reflects the ancestor's circumstances, not the courthouse's losses.
source_references: Tennessee State Library and Archives. Guide to County Records in the Tennessee State Library and Archives. Nashville: TSLA.
Waldrep, Christopher. Night Riders: Defending Community in the Black Patch, 1890-1915. Durham: Duke University Press, 1993.
Sistler, Byron, and Barbara Sistler. Early Middle Tennessee Marriages. Nashville: Byron Sistler & Associates, 1988.
